summary.title: Systematic review of self-supervised foundation models for brain network representation using electroencephalography

papers.title: Systematic review of self-supervised foundation models for brain network representation using electroencephalography

reasons: Explicitly titled as a systematic review of self-supervised foundation models for EEG.
Focuses on pretrained reusable EEG representations and their adaptation to downstream tasks.
Analyzes the EEG-FM ecosystem, including architectures, SSL objectives, and datasets.

summary.detailed_summary: ZUNA is a masked diffusion autoencoder designed for EEG superresolution and channel reconstruction across arbitrary electrode configurations. It introduces a 4D rotary positional encoding (RoPE) to handle spatial (x,y,z) and temporal (t) coordinates, enabling generalization to novel channel positions. Trained on 2 million channel-hours from 208 datasets, the model significantly outperforms spherical-spline interpolation, particularly in low-density or high-dropout scenarios.

headline_results: Significantly outperforms MNE spherical-spline interpolation in NMSE across all tested datasets.
Performance gap increases as the number of missing channels increases.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: ZUNA is a 380M-parameter diffusion autoencoder capable of reconstructing missing EEG channels and upsampling spatial resolution.
Novel 4D-RoPE architecture enables the model to handle arbitrary channel subsets and positions without requiring a fixed montage.
Trained on a massive corpus of 2 million channel-hours, it consistently outperforms standard spherical-spline interpolation, especially at high upsampling factors.
limitations: Potential to hallucinate incorrect EEG signals due to its generative nature.
Limited context length of 5 seconds per sample.
Only utilizes time-domain signals, ignoring other potential input features.
Data scale is small compared to other modalities like vision or text.

method.architecture: Transformer-based encoder-decoder diffusion autoencoder using rectified flow loss and 4D rotary positional embeddings (x,y,z,t).
method.objective: Masked reconstruction with a heavy channel-dropout (90%) rate.
method.pretraining: Trained on 2 million channel-hours of EEG data from 208 public datasets.

summary.one_liner: A 380M-parameter diffusion autoencoder for flexible EEG channel infilling and superresolution using 4D rotary positional embeddings.
summary.title: ZUNA: Flexible EEG Superresolution with Position-Aware Diffusion Autoencoders
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a 4D rotary positional encoding scheme that allows an EEG foundation model to generalize across arbitrary electrode numbers and 3D scalp positions.

papers.title: ZUNA: Flexible EEG Superresolution with Position-Aware Diffusion Autoencoders

reasons: Trained on a massive aggregated corpus of 208 public datasets containing approximately 2 million channel-hours.
Designed for generalizability across arbitrary electrode positions and novel datasets.
Explicitly releases pretrained weights for downstream use in EEG analysis pipelines.
Large-scale 380M-parameter model utilizing a masked diffusion autoencoder for broad EEG representation.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper introduces Brain4FMs, an open evaluation platform that integrates 15 representative brain foundation models (BFMs) and 18 public datasets to standardize the assessment of electrical brain signal decoding. It proposes a unified SSL taxonomy categorizing BFMs into contrastive, generative, and other advanced paradigms. Through extensive cross-subject evaluation across 11 tasks, the authors analyze how pretraining modalities, SSL objectives, and architectural choices affect downstream generalization and performance.

headline_results: Generative models generally outperform contrastive ones on most downstream tasks.
Bidirectional AE-style models outperform unidirectional AR-style models for classification.
CPC-based contrastive models are stronger than augmentation-based ones.

key_points: Benchmark for EEG foundation models: Integrates 15 BFMs and 18 public datasets across 11 tasks, including disease diagnosis, sleep staging, communication, and affective computing.
SSL strategy analysis: Finds that generative models generally outperform contrastive ones, and CPC-based methods are superior to augmentation-based contrastive learning for clinical tasks.
Architectural insights: Demonstrates that bidirectional autoencoder representations are more effective for classification than unidirectional autoregressive ones.
limitations: Performance on communication and affective computing tasks remains limited due to high cross-subject variability.
Pronounced modality dependence observed, where iEEG-pretrained models perform poorly on EEG datasets.
Evaluation is currently limited to classification tasks; frozen-encoder and zero-shot settings are not yet implemented.
Codebook discretization may constrain discriminative flexibility and discard fine-grained cues during finetuning.

method.architecture: Evaluates a variety of backbones including Transformers, CNNs, GNNs, and Mamba.
method.finetuning: Cross-subject leave-subjects-out protocol with task-specific linear or MLP classifiers.
method.objective: Analyzes Contrastive (Augmentation, CPC, Cross-modal) and Generative (AR, AE) objectives.
method.pretraining: Standardized SSL pretraining across 15 BFMs using diverse brain signal datasets.

summary.one_liner: A standardized evaluation platform and SSL-centric taxonomy for benchmarking 15 brain foundation models across 18 public EEG/iEEG datasets.
summary.title: Brain4FMs: A Benchmark of Foundation Models for Electrical Brain Signal
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a unified SSL-centric taxonomy and a plug-and-play benchmarking platform for the systematic evaluation of brain foundation models across diverse clinical and human-centric tasks.

papers.title: Brain4FMs: A Benchmark of Foundation Models for Electrical Brain Signal

reasons: Explicitly targets Brain Foundation Models (BFMs) with a focus on EEG and intracranial EEG.
Introduces Brain4FMs, a standardized evaluation platform and benchmark for 15 representative BFMs.
Analyzes the impact of pretraining data and SSL strategies on generalization and downstream performance.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes sleep2vec, a foundation model designed to align nine different nocturnal biosignals (including EEG, ECG, and SpO2) into a shared embedding space. Unlike prior work focusing on reconstruction or limited channel sets, sleep2vec uses a metadata-aware contrastive objective (DASH-InfoNCE) to improve robustness across diverse recording sites and demographics. Evaluation on sleep staging and clinical diagnosis tasks across SHHS and WSC datasets demonstrates that the model outperforms existing foundation models and remains robust to sensor dropout.

headline_results: Outperforms SleepFM and SleepFounder in cross-cohort sleep staging.
Maintains high performance despite significant sensor dropout.
Demonstrates scaling laws regarding modality diversity and model capacity.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: sleep2vec aligns nine heterogeneous nocturnal biosignals into a unified representation space using a RoFormer backbone.
Introduces DASH-InfoNCE, which uses age, gender, and site metadata to dynamically weight negatives, reducing overfitting to cohort-specific shortcuts.
Demonstrates clear scaling laws where increasing modality diversity and model capacity (up to 238M parameters) consistently improves cross-cohort generalization.
limitations: The Gating Mechanism for feature fusion does not capture higher-order interactions between modalities.
Scaling effects exhibit diminishing returns as model size increases.
Performance on some specialized sleep staging tasks still slightly trails the best task-specific non-FM models.
The DASH-InfoNCE objective relies on the availability of demographic and site metadata during pre-training.

method.architecture: Modality-specific MLP tokenizers feeding into a modality-agnostic RoFormer backbone.
method.finetuning: LoRA with a Gating Mechanism for modality feature fusion.
method.objective: DASH-InfoNCE (Demography, Age, Site & History-aware InfoNCE) contrastive loss.
method.pretraining: Cross-modal alignment of 9 biosignals using metadata-weighted negatives.

summary.one_liner: A multimodal foundation model for nocturnal biosignals that uses metadata-aware contrastive alignment to handle heterogeneous sensor sets and missing modalities.
summary.title: sleep2vec: Unified Cross-Modal Alignment for Heterogeneous Nocturnal Biosignals
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces DASH-InfoNCE, a contrastive objective that leverages demographic and acquisition metadata to weight negative samples and mitigate cohort-specific shortcuts.

papers.title: sleep2vec: Unified Cross-Modal Alignment for Heterogeneous Nocturnal Biosignals

reasons: Explicitly describes sleep2vec as a 'foundation model for diverse and incomplete nocturnal biosignals'.
Uses large-scale contrastive pre-training on 42,249 recordings across multiple modalities, including EEG.
Demonstrates transferability to downstream tasks such as sleep staging and clinical outcome assessment.
Includes scaling laws analysis for nocturnal biosignals, fitting the EEG-FM ecosystem criteria.

datasets: Temple University Hospital EEG Corpus (TUEG) v2.0.1

summary.detailed_summary: BrainRVQ proposes a general-purpose EEG foundation model that utilizes a Dual-Domain Residual Vector Quantization (DD-RVQ) tokenizer to disentangle temporal waveforms and spectral patterns into hierarchical discrete codes. It employs a hierarchical autoregressive pre-training objective and an importance-guided curriculum masking strategy to prioritize information-rich neural events over background noise. The model was pre-trained on approximately 9,200 hours of clinical EEG data and demonstrated superior performance across 8 diverse downstream tasks, including seizure detection and motor imagery.
key_points: New EEG foundation model: BrainRVQ uses dual-domain residual quantization to encode both temporal transients and spectral oscillations into hierarchical discrete tokens.
Implements a hierarchical autoregressive objective and importance-guided curriculum masking to learn coarse-to-fine neural representations while suppressing noise.
Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across 8 diverse downstream datasets, showing strong generalization in clinical and BCI applications.
limitations: Pre-trained on data from a single medical center, potentially limiting cross-site generalization
Relies on a fixed 19-channel 10-20 system configuration
Limited to EEG signals, excluding other modalities like EOG or EMG

method.architecture: Transformer encoder with a DD-RVQ tokenizer
method.finetuning: Task-specific MLP heads with layer-wise learning rate decay
method.objective: Hierarchical autoregressive reconstruction of discrete tokens
method.pretraining: Self-supervised pre-training on TUEG using importance-guided curriculum masking

summary.one_liner: A high-fidelity EEG foundation model using dual-domain residual quantization and hierarchical autoregressive pre-training to capture complex spectro-temporal neural dynamics.
summary.title: BrainRVQ: A High-Fidelity EEG Foundation Model via Dual-Domain Residual Quantization and Hierarchical Autoregression
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a dual-domain residual vector quantization (DD-RVQ) tokenizer and a hierarchical autoregressive objective to model the coarse-to-fine structure of EEG signals across both time and frequency domains.

papers.title: BrainRVQ: A High-Fidelity EEG Foundation Model via Dual-Domain Residual Quantization and Hierarchical Autoregression

reasons: Explicitly identifies as a 'general-purpose EEG foundation model'
Pre-trained on a large-scale corpus of clinical EEG data
Demonstrates broad transferability across 8 diverse downstream datasets

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes SCOPE, a framework designed to address the generalization gap of EEG foundation models (EFMs) when subject-level supervision is scarce. It first constructs reliable pseudo-labels by fusing predictions from an ETF-regularized task-prior network and Sinkhorn-constrained class prototypes using Dempster-Shafer theory. These signals then guide a lightweight ProAdapter that modulates the frozen EFM's deeper layers via feature-wise modulation. Evaluation across five EFM backbones and three tasks (sleep staging, emotion, and workload) shows that SCOPE consistently outperforms full fine-tuning and frozen baselines while using only 2-5% of trainable parameters.

headline_results: Outperforms full fine-tuning and LoRA under 30% subject-label limit.
Achieves superior performance using only 2-5% of trainable parameters.

key_points: New adaptation method: SCOPE framework for EEG foundation models to enable robust cross-subject generalization with limited labeled data.
Combines ETF-regularized task priors and prototype clustering with Dempster-Shafer fusion to generate high-confidence pseudo-labels.
Employs a prototype-conditioned adapter (ProAdapter) to modulate frozen EFM layers, achieving superior performance over full fine-tuning with minimal parameter overhead.
limitations: Performance is sensitive to the selection of the confidence threshold rho.
Requires a small amount of labeled data to initialize the Task-Prior Network.
Optimal adapter depth L may vary across different EFM architectures.
Evaluation is limited to three public datasets, which may not represent all clinical EEG scenarios.

method.architecture: Two-stage framework consisting of an ETF-regularized Task-Prior Network (TPN) and a prototype-conditioned adapter (ProAdapter) inserted into the last L layers of a frozen EFM.
method.finetuning: Warm-up on labeled data followed by semi-supervised updates using confidence-aware pseudo-labels.
method.objective: Confidence-weighted semi-supervised loss using pseudo-labels fused via Dempster-Shafer theory.
method.pretraining: Uses frozen pre-trained EFMs (e.g., LaBraM, EEGMamba) as backbones.

summary.one_liner: A two-stage adaptation framework for EEG foundation models that uses prototype-guided supervision and lightweight adapters to improve generalization under limited label conditions.
summary.title: Structured Prototype-Guided Adaptation for EEG Foundation Models
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a structured, confidence-aware prototype-guided adaptation framework (SCOPE) that leverages external task priors and prototype-conditioned adapters to stabilize EFM fine-tuning with limited subject-level labels.

papers.title: Structured Prototype-Guided Adaptation for EEG Foundation Models

reasons: Explicitly targets EEG foundation models (EFMs) in both title and abstract.
Proposes a specific adaptation framework (SCOPE) for EFM fine-tuning.
Evaluates the method across five different foundation model backbones.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes NeuroNarrator, a multimodal large language model designed for segment-level clinical EEG interpretation. It introduces NeuroCorpus-160K, a large-scale dataset of 160k EEG-text pairs, and employs a dual-stream encoder to align temporal waveforms with spatial topographic maps via a contrastive objective. The model integrates historical temporal context through a state-space-inspired formulation to generate coherent narratives, demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization and clinical fidelity across diverse benchmarks.
key_points: New EEG foundation model: NeuroNarrator translates EEG segments into detailed clinical narratives by bridging continuous signal dynamics and discrete language.
Employs a dual-stream architecture that aligns temporal EEG waveforms with spatial topographic maps using a contrastive objective and conditions a Qwen3-4B LLM on historical state-space trajectories.
Demonstrates robust clinical fidelity and zero-shot transfer capabilities across multiple external datasets, including epilepsy and depression detection.
limitations: Lower precision in extracting specific 'Event & Clinical Label' compared to closed-set discriminative baselines.
Lack of principled, physiology-aware evaluation metrics for open-ended clinical descriptions.
Zero-shot performance is limited by the current scaling regime and distributional shifts.
Training corpus (NeuroCorpus-160K) relies on GPT-4 for refining structured templates into narratives.

method.architecture: Dual-stream encoder (LaBraM-Base for temporal, CLIP ViT-Large for spatial) projected into a shared space and fed into a Qwen3-4B-Instruct LLM.
method.finetuning: Joint optimization of EEG encoder, projectors, and LLM backbone.
method.objective: SigLIP-inspired contrastive alignment for spectro-spatial grounding and negative log-likelihood for autoregressive text generation.
method.pretraining: Contrastive alignment followed by supervised fine-tuning on NeuroCorpus-160K.

summary.one_liner: A generalist EEG-to-text foundation model that translates electrophysiological segments into clinically grounded narratives using spectro-spatial alignment and temporal state-space reasoning.

open_source.license: Available from corresponding author upon reasonable request

summary.title: NeuroNarrator: A Generalist EEG-to-Text Foundation Model for Clinical Interpretation via Spectro-Spatial Grounding and Temporal State-Space Reasoning
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces the first generalist EEG-to-text foundation model and a large-scale harmonized corpus (NeuroCorpus-160K) for open-vocabulary clinical interpretation of EEG segments.

papers.title: NeuroNarrator: A Generalist EEG-to-Text Foundation Model for Clinical Interpretation via Spectro-Spatial Grounding and Temporal State-Space Reasoning

reasons: Explicitly identifies as a 'generalist EEG-to-text foundation model'
Utilizes a large-scale dataset (NeuroCorpus-160K) for pretraining
Demonstrates zero-shot transfer capabilities across diverse benchmarks
Focuses on creating a foundational framework for open-ended clinical interpretation

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes Brain-OF, a foundation model that handles heterogeneous brain signals by projecting them into a shared semantic space via the Any-Resolution Neural Signal Sampler (ARNESS). It employs a backbone combining DINT attention and a Sparse Mixture of Experts (SMoE) to balance modality-invariant and modality-specific features. The model is pretrained using Masked Temporal-Frequency Modeling (MTFM) on 37 datasets and demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across seven downstream tasks, including disease diagnosis and brain age prediction.

headline_results: Brain-OF Huge ranked 1st in 7 out of 9 tasks.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: Brain-OF integrates EEG, MEG, and fMRI into a single framework to exploit complementary spatiotemporal dynamics.
Introduces ARNESS for resolution-agnostic sampling and MTFM for joint reconstruction in both time and frequency domains.
Achieves state-of-the-art results on 7 out of 9 evaluated tasks, demonstrating strong cross-modal generalization and label efficiency.
limitations: Overall data scale is significantly smaller than NLP or CV foundation models.
Notable modality imbalance, with fMRI samples being scarcer than EEG and MEG.
Multimodal fusion strategy is relatively straightforward and may not capture complex nonlinear interactions.
Downstream evaluation relies on full finetuning, which is computationally expensive.

method.architecture: 1D Conv encoder followed by ARNESS (Perceiver-style cross-attention) and a backbone consisting of DINT attention and Sparse MoE.
method.finetuning: Full finetuning with a linear head.
method.objective: Masked Temporal-Frequency Modeling (MTFM) for joint time-frequency reconstruction.
method.pretraining: Jointly on fMRI, EEG, and MEG.

summary.one_liner: A multimodal foundation model jointly pretrained on fMRI, EEG, and MEG using a shared semantic space and dual-domain reconstruction.
summary.title: Brain-OF: An Omnifunctional Foundation Model for fMRI, EEG and MEG
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces an omnifunctional framework that integrates fMRI, EEG, and MEG through a resolution-agnostic sampler and a joint time-frequency pretraining objective.

papers.title: Brain-OF: An Omnifunctional Foundation Model for fMRI, EEG and MEG

reasons: Explicitly proposes a brain foundation model (Brain-OF) with EEG as a core target modality.
Pretrained on a large-scale corpus of 40 datasets for broad transfer to diverse downstream tasks.
Introduces foundation-model specific architectures and pretraining objectives like Masked Temporal-Frequency Modeling.

summary.title: CAMEL-CLIP: Channel-aware Multimodal Electroencephalography-text Alignment for Generalizable Brain Foundation Models

papers.title: CAMEL-CLIP: Channel-aware Multimodal Electroencephalography-text Alignment for Generalizable Brain Foundation Models

reasons: Explicitly proposes a 'contrastive EEG-text multimodal foundation model' for generalizable representations.
Targets broad applicability across 'diverse downstream tasks' using linear-probing.
Directly addresses challenges in EEG foundation models, specifically channel heterogeneity.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes PRISM, a masked autoencoder for EEG that compares narrow-source (EU/US) versus geographically diverse (including South Asian) pretraining corpora. It reveals a trade-off where narrow-source data favors linear probing on matched benchmarks, while diverse data enhances performance under fine-tuning, particularly for a novel epilepsy vs. mimickers differential diagnosis task. The authors also provide a systematic decomposition of six methodological inconsistencies between existing EEG-FM benchmarks that can reverse model rankings.

headline_results: Diverse pretraining improves epilepsy vs. mimickers task by 12.3 pp balanced accuracy.
PRISM matches or outperforms REVE on most benchmarks despite smaller dataset count.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: PRISM uses a masked autoencoder with 4D positional encoding to learn representations across heterogeneous populations.
Evidence shows that diverse pretraining improves fine-tuning adaptability and outperforms narrow-source models by 12.3 pp on an epilepsy vs. mimickers task.
Analysis reveals that dataset count is often a confounding variable and identifies six sources of inconsistency between major EEG-FM benchmarks.
limitations: Only a single model size was explored, leaving scaling effects unknown.
Geographic diversity is confounded with acquisition system diversity.
The epilepsy-mimicker dataset is limited to South Asian clinical centers.
The masking ratio was kept fixed during experiments.
Generalizability of the MLP head advantage to other architectures requires further investigation.

method.architecture: Masked autoencoder with a 12-layer transformer encoder and 4-layer decoder; employs 4D positional encoding (spatial coordinates + time).
method.finetuning: Linear probing and full fine-tuning on downstream benchmarks and clinical tasks.
method.objective: Dual L1 reconstruction loss consisting of a primary decoder loss and an auxiliary global embedding loss.
method.pretraining: Pretrained on narrow-source (EU/US) and diverse-source (EU/US/South Asian) corpora.

summary.one_liner: Introduces PRISM, a masked autoencoder that demonstrates how targeted population diversity in pretraining improves clinical transferability and can substitute for massive data scale.
summary.title: PRISM: Exploring Heterogeneous Pretrained EEG Foundation Model Transfer to Clinical Differential Diagnosis
summary.unique_contribution: Demonstrates that targeted population diversity in pretraining can substitute for indiscriminate data scale and significantly improves performance on challenging clinical differential diagnosis tasks.

papers.title: PRISM: Exploring Heterogeneous Pretrained EEG Foundation Model Transfer to Clinical Differential Diagnosis

reasons: Explicitly focuses on EEG foundation models and their transferability to clinical diagnosis.
Introduces PRISM, a pretrained masked autoencoder designed for reusable EEG representations.
Compares performance against other EEG-FMs (REVE) and analyzes EEG-FM benchmarks.
Systematically evaluates the impact of pretraining population diversity on downstream adaptation.
